records.ety: [F. sceptre, L. sceptrum, from Gr. <?/ a staff to lean upon, a scepter; probably akin to E. shaft. See Shaft, and cf. Scape a stem, shaft.]

defs: A staff or baton borne by a sovereign, as a ceremonial badge or emblem of authority; a royal mace.
quotes: And the king held out Esther the golden scepter that was in his hand.

defs: Hence, royal or imperial power or authority; sovereignty; as, to assume the scepter.
quotes: The scepter shall not depart from Judah, nor a lawgiver from between his feet, until Shiloh come.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Sceptered (?) or Sceptred (<?/); p. pr. & vb. n. Sceptering (?) or Sceptring (<?/).]

defs: To endow with the scepter, or emblem of authority; to invest with royal authority.
quotes: To Britain's queen the sceptered suppliant bends.
